issue_title: Mandate creation deletes port in return_url
issue_body:
When creating a Mandate, the `return_url` port is deleted when the object is saved.

```python
>> from mangopay.resources import Mandate, User

>> mandate = Mandate(user=[User],
                     bank_account_id=[bank_account_id],
                     culture='FR',
                     return_url='http://127.0.0.1:8080/test')
            
>> print(mangopay_mandate.return_url)
http://127.0.0.1:8080/test

>> mangopay_mandate.save()

>> print(mangopay_mandate.return_url)
http://127.0.0.1/test
```

The behevior is not the same for Web PayIn where ports are kept.

issue_title: Methods suppose to return enum is returning Int

read_status method suppose to return acquisition status as enum type . Instead it is returning Int.
 
```
with nidmm.Session(args.name) as session:
     print(session.read_status())
     print(type(session.read_status()[0]))
     print(type(session.read_status()[1]))
 
>>> 
(0, 4)
<class 'int'>
<class 'int'>
```

issue_title: Blocking call when using ProgressiveResult (asyncio)
issue_body:
* What I want to do: send a  heavy payload to a remote component using the progressive result.

* What I do: The sender creates a function to send the payload by chunk, register that function with a temp uri then call a `receive` rpc registered by the Receiver that take the temp uri in parameter. the `receive` function got the chunk send by the Sender and reconstruct the payload once all pieces have been received.

* What happens: the sender block after it calls the `receive` rcp. ie it blocks while sending stuff if details.progress is not None.

* Minimal example: https://gist.github.com/rdebroiz/0143ab027c17cc641ad20cea5238821c

Start first a receiver
```sh
python autobahn_progressive_send_block.py receive
```
then a sender
```sh
python autobahn_progressive_send_block.py send
```
The receiver call a rcp to the sender that just print "pouet" each time it receive a chunk.

On the receiver console all works as expected. On the sender console no ping/pong  message are received until all the chunk have been sent, all the pouet message arrived when all the chunk have been sent as well

* What I would expect: receive pouet and ping/pong messages while it is still sending chunks. But the ping/pong just stop until sending all the chunks is finish, then it comes back and the sender receive all the pouet message.

Running a `asyncio.wamp.ApplicationSession` with `ApplicationRunner.run(session start_loop=True)`
two consecutive times fails the second one.

Minimal example: https://gist.github.com/rdebroiz/1854c2bab7da929e376c273d262c805a

Adding
``` python
if loop.is_closed():
    asyncio.set_event_loop(asyncio.new_event_loop())
```
Before to run the ApplicationRunner the second times make it works as expected.

This code snippet is coming from and is already called by `asyncio.wamp.ApplicationRunner.run`
see: https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/asyncio/wamp.py#L246

Any ideas of what is going on?

issue_title: k8s: Use Downward API to expose namespace, pod name
issue_body:
It is probably a good practice for us to use the Kubernetes Downward API to add some environment variables into the container. Some simple changes to the pod spec in the rc would get us some useful information.

Downward API:
http://kubernetes.io/v1.0/docs/user-guide/downward-api/
Example: 
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/kubernetes/blob/master/docs/user-guide/downward-api/dapi-pod.yaml

Things I think we should probably expose:

- APP_NAME (pod name)
- APP_NAMESPACE (pod namespace)
- APP_LABELS (if we can get these into a pretty list)

Are there other pieces of metadata that might be good to add? Is there a way we can get the Docker image and label names? That would be useful for debugging.

If we're going to quickly switch the app name to be the same as DEIS_APP, then APP_NAME is probably redundant.

issue_title: growth strategy for unknown parameter types does not work with EXASOL
issue_body:
If you execute a parametrised query where EXASOL does not know exactly the type, like:
`cursor.execute("SELECT x from (values(1), (2)) as tbl (x) where x = ?;" , [2,])`
turbodbc allocates a huge amount of memory. This combined with the default value for parameter_sets_to_buffer could lead to a MemoryError.

issue_title: Windows uninstall is slow
issue_body:
I'm submitting a bug report for the Anaconda Windows installer, I don't know if this is the right place.  But first I'd like to thank the team for providing such great windows support for Anaconda.  Awesome job.

The bug I think is that the uninstaller tries to print too much stuff to the screen, causing it to be very slow at deleting files.  So this UI takes for ever:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18707114/35555347-b5be5e04-0553-11e8-9546-9cf2c7ac637e.png)

## Current Behavior

The files are flashing by quickly but there are so many of them that the UI DrawText routine is taking all the time.  If one tries "rd /s /q" the folder can be deleted much more quickly than the Installer leads one to believe...

## Steps to Reproduce
Create 5 environments, each with lots of packages (100 or so), then uninstall Anaconda (conda install Jupyter is a good way to get lots of packages).

## Expected Behavior
The uninstaller UI could "self-throttle" the output by drawing on a separate rending thread at 30 fps whatever file name is currently being deleted, this will decouple the UI performance from the File system delete operation, and make the delete much faster.

issue_title: After a fresh install of Miniconda running `conda install jupyter` causes future use to result in [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED]
issue_body:
After successfully running `conda install jupyter` on a fresh install of Miniconda I receive [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] errors on all further attempts to use conda.

This is on Windows 7 64 bit.

My only means of currently installing anything is to run `conda config --set ssl_verify False`.

issue_title: RFE: Interpolating on key level
issue_body:
Given that ini file:
```
[DEFAULT]
foo = 42

[s]
bar = 43
baz = %(foo)s
```

ConfigParser is able to return a value for key `foo` in section `s`:
```
print c.get('s', 'bar')  # 43
print c.get('s', 'baz')  # 42
print c.get('s', 'foo')  # 42
```

Using `ConfigObj` however, a `KeyError` is thrown:
```
print c['s']['bar']  # 43
print c['s']['baz']  # 42
print c['s']['foo']  # KeyError 'foo'
```

`ConfigParser`'s behavior is quite handy in some situations, as it allows to 'inherit' keys from a default section.

Any chance this could be implemented?

issue_title: [Bug] Shared libraries for GnuCOBOL are copied to source directory!!!
issue_body:
This was likely added for solving some bug but the current behaviour is a bug itself - Nobody wants to pollute (every?) source directory with runtime dlls.

issue_title: Add --debug argument
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm looking for a way to selectively print debug output for the simulator setup, like the command line used etc.

There is the `--verbose` argument but that does seems to be reserved to control the verbosity of the simulation run itself. So I'd propose a globally available `--debug` argument.

issue_title: Curator Beginner Here
issue_body:
Hello,

Just installed Curator 4.1.2 on my Ubuntu 15.10 ElasticSearch/Kibana Server. 
Just a little confused on how to set this up and the ElasticSearch guide is not very clear on what it is I need to do in order to setup Curator to simply remove Indices 60 days or older.  I mean I see here: https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/ex_delete_indices.html 

But this really doesn't tell me how to set it up? I simply installed it via the APT Repository and from there doesn't really say where to get an "action file" and the "configuration file" and I checked the home folder and /opt/elasticsearch-curator but no sign of those files. . Is this something I need to write myself? All I need to do really is delete indices on my ElasticSearch Server for 60 days and older (pretty simple no?) I can see all my dashboards on Kibana just fine but running out of space quite quickly.

Sorry this is very intro and I apologize if this is technically not an issue but I really didn't know where else to go.

Thanks

issue_title: Test timeout issues in Jenkins
issue_body:
This:

> ```
ConnectionTimeout caused by - ReadTimeoutError(HTTPConnectionPool(host=u'localhost', port=9220): Read timed out. (read timeout=10))
```

is being caused by https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/test/integration/__init__.py#L32:

> ```
client = Elasticsearch([os.environ.get('TEST_ES_SERVER', {})])
```

A high timeout needs to be added here to prevent test timeouts.

issue_title: Elasticsearch.search() unrecognized parameter: [analyzer]
issue_body:
The Elasticsearch-py API Document says that search(*args, **kwargs) function has a parameter called 'analyzer'.
But the following code raise a RequestError:TransportError(400, 'illegal_argument_exception', 'request [/test-index/content-field/_search] contains unrecognized parameter: [analyzer]').
`from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch`
`from elasticsearch.client import IndicesClient`
`es=Elasticsearch()`
`res= es.search(index="test-index", doc_type='content-field',body={"query": {"match": {"text": "微观文明"}}},analyzer="ik_smart")`

PS: I am using version 5.0.1 for Elasticsearch-py and version 5.0.0 for elasticsearch.

issue_title: OpenTracing support
issue_body:
Was wondering about any chance to natively support OpenTracing (http://opentracing.io/), which is a technology to do distributed tracing, with support for many languages and ongoing efforts to support many libraries (like SQLAlchemy, or Django).

If that, I could probably create a patch, given the instructions/discussion - else, in order to trace the execution of requests done to the actual ES server, I'd like to create a patch to either have event handling (say, "perform_request" event). If this sounds more feasible, let me know as well ;)

(Right now, the way I found to do this was to create subclass `Transport`, override `perform_request`, do a custom tracing (and calculating the time the request took), and then simply call the base implementation).

Any feedback is welcome. Thanks!

issue_title: Make examples more visually distinguishable
issue_body:
I want to provide several examples for a new update command that I'm working on, but the list of examples is visually difficult to read due to them being all clumped together. See below for an idea of what I mean. The examples just look like an ugly blob of text.

Perhaps just add a blank line between example text and the next example's title?

```
Command
    az sql db audit-policy update: Updates a database's auditing policy.
        If the policy is being enabled, storage_account or both storage_endpoint and
        storage_account_access_key must be specified.

Arguments
    --name -n           [Required]: Name of the Azure SQL Database.
    --resource-group -g [Required]: Name of resource group. You can configure the default group
                                    using 'az configure --defaults group=<name>'.
    --server -s         [Required]: Name of the Azure SQL server.

Policy Arguments
    --actions                     : List of actions and action groups to audit.
    --retention-days              : The number of days to retain audit logs.
    --state                       : Auditing policy state.

Storage Arguments
    --storage-account             : Name of the storage account.
    --storage-endpoint            : The storage account endpoint.
    --storage-key                 : Access key for the storage account.
    --storage-resource-group      : Resource group containing the storage account. If unspecified,
                                    defaults to the database's resource group.

Generic Update Arguments
    --add                         : Add an object to a list of objects by specifying a path and key
                                    value pairs.  Example: --add property.listProperty <key=value,
                                    string or JSON string>.
    --remove                      : Remove a property or an element from a list.  Example: --remove
                                    property.list <indexToRemove> OR --remove propertyToRemove.
    --set                         : Update an object by specifying a property path and value to set
                                    Example: --set property1.property2=<value>.

Global Arguments
    --debug                       : Increase logging verbosity to show all debug logs.
    --help -h                     : Show this help message and exit.
    --output -o                   : Output format.  Allowed values: json, jsonc, table, tsv.
                                    Default: json.
    --query                       : JMESPath query string. See http://jmespath.org/ for more
                                    information and examples.
    --verbose                     : Increase logging verbosity. Use --debug for full debug logs.

Examples
    Enable an auditing policy by specifying storage account name
        az db audit-policy update -g mygroup -s myserver -d mydb --state Enabled --storage-account
        mystorage
    Enable an auditing policy by specifying storage account name and resource group
        az db audit-policy update -g mygroup -s myserver -d mydb --state Enabled --storage-account
        mystorage --storage-resource-group mygroup2
    Enable an auditing policy by specifying storage endpoint and key
        az db audit-policy update -g mygroup -s myserver -d mydb --state Enabled --storage-endpoint
        https://mystorage.blob.core.windows.net --storage-key MYKEY==
    Set the list of audit actions
        az db audit-policy update -g mygroup -s myserver -d mydb --actions
        FAILED_DATABASE_AUTHENTICATION_GROUP 'UPDATE on database::mydb by public'
    Add an audit action
        az db audit-policy update -g mygroup -s myserver -d mydb --add auditActionsAndGroups
        FAILED_DATABASE_AUTHENTICATION_GROUP
    Remove an audit action by list index
        az db audit-policy update -g mygroup -s myserver -d mydb --add auditActionsAndGroups 0
    Disable an auditing policy
        az db audit-policy update -g mygroup -s myserver -d mydb --state Disabled
```

issue_title: Extend StreamResponse documentation
issue_body:
## Long story short

I need to stream content from given endpoint read from other source.
For that purpose I'm leveraging `aiohttp.web.StreamResponse` class.
Streaming work well, but can't be finalized when stream is closed from client side.

Documentation does not have example how to handle case - when client interrupted the stream.
I have found `StreamResponse.task` field. But it not seems to be usable for this purpose.

So I'm asking aiohttp team to extend documentation and show how to deal with this.

## Expected behaviour

- Documentation describe how to handle StreamResponse interrupts from client side.
- Endpoint handler ends properly in snippet below.

## Actual behaviour

- Documentation **does not** describe how to handle StreamResponse interrupts from client side.
- Endpoint handler enters in infinite loop in snippet below.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Run snippet bellow.
2. Send request to the endpoint.

### Snippet
```py

from aiohttp import web


async def infinite_stream(request):
    stream = web.StreamResponse()
    await stream.prepare(request)
    while True:
        if stream.task.done() or stream.task.cancelled():
            break
        await stream.write(b'Hello Aiohttp!')
    # <<<==== Lines below are never executed
    await stream.write_eof()
    return stream


def main():
    app = web.Application()
    app.router.add_get('/infinite-stream', infinite_stream)
    web.run_app(app, host='127.0.0.1', port=8080)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

```

### Command to send request
```sh
wget http://127.0.0.1:8080/infinite-stream
```

## Your environment

Ubuntu 17.04, Proxy are not used, Python 3.6, aiohttp 2.2.5, pip 9.0.1

issue_title: Cancel on waiting jobs should be protected
issue_body:
Example:

```python
from aiohttp import web
from aiojobs.aiohttp import setup, spawn

async def handler(request):
    await spawn(request, coro())
    return web.Response()

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/', handler)
setup(app)
```

Cancellation of `await spawn(request, coro())` by https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/pull/2257/files should be covered by `asyncio,shield` or custom wrapper

As well aiohttp docs for now can use combination of `asyncio.shield` and `spawn` to illuminate unexpected behavior

issue_title: automatic scrolling to bottom of next cell is bad when next cell is very long
issue_body:
Currently, in my Jupyter notebook (`pip install -U jupyter`), whenever I run a cell, the notebook automatically scrolls to the bottom of the next cell. Presumedly, this is useful if I'm running several cells in a row, but oftentimes, I'm in the situation where I have:

```
-----
small cell here
-----

-----
small output or plot here
-----

-----
HUGE cell here
-----
```

and I want to modify/run/modify/run the small cell various times. It is a HUGE hassle to have to scroll up to click on the cell I want and edit again, let along simply to see the output/plot that I was looking for. 

Maybe scrolling should first check if scrolling as it currently does would send the previous cell off-screen, and, if so, make sure that the top of the screen is instead aligned either with the top of the output of the just-executed cell or maybe some predefined (min(10% of screen height, height of 5 lines of text)) above that? 

I know that complicating the auto-scrolling effect could be undesireable, since it might violate the principle of least-confusion if we're not careful, but almost anything (even no scrolling) is better than the current behavior in the use case that I mentioned. 

This is especially annoying when making very complicated Matplotlib-based plots, as the code to generate these can often grow into 100+ lines and, especially during development, you'll want to tweak various parts of the code without separating it into a function with fixed parameters.

thoughts?

issue_title: supply a stamp file to peru, which can be touched if there are changes
issue_body:
When calling from `make`, you usually need to touch a fake build stamp file to indicate that the peru rule has run. Otherwise there's no way for make to know whether it's run before or not, and then every other rule that depends on it will get rerun every time. Not ideal for builds where a no-op make still takes a while or produces a bunch of output.

But using a build stamp file is kind of annoying too, because it means peru isn't checking your dependencies for (evil) changes. If you want that behavior, you need a way to run peru and have it actually *tell you* whether any dependency files were changed. It might be nice to have a command line option that pointed to a file that could be `touch`'ed for this purpose.

Creating a new release
----------------------

Scikit-build releases are uploaded to `PyPI <https://pypi.python.org/pypi/scikit-build>`_
for easy installation via ``pip``. In addition, the python source packages
are stored as releases inside the official
`github repository <https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/releases>`_. The
recommended process for generating a new release is described here.

1.  From the target commit, set the desired version number in ``setup.py``. 
    Create a new commit and note the SHA; this will become the release tag.

2.  Ensure that all tests pass. (AppVeyor, CircleCI and TravisCI should be green)

3.  Run ``python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel``.  This will generate the source
    distribution tarball and wheel in the ``dist`` directory with names like
    ``scikit-build-<version>.tar.gz`` and ``scikit_build-<version>-py2.py3-none-any.whl``.

4.  Create a new virtual environment and install the python package into
    it. This should not be done in the repository
    directory because the wrong ``skbuild`` package would be imported.  ::

        mkdir test && cd test
        virtualenv release
        source release/bin/activate
        pip install ../dist/scikit_build-<version>-py2.py3-none-any.whl

5.  Now you can try to import skbuild.

        python -c "import skbuild"

6. Create a ``~/.pypirc`` 
    consisting of: ::

        [distutils]
        index-servers=
            test

        [test]
        repository = https://testpypi.python.org/pypi
        username = <your user name goes here>
        password = <your password goes here>


Then use ::

        python setup.py register -r test

to register your project with the PyPI test server.

And finally, upload the release to PyPI test server using ``twine``: ::

        pip install twine
        twine upload -r test -s dist/scikit*

7. Test that the release can be installed: ::

        deactivate
        virtualenv install
        source install/bin/activate
        pip install -i https://testpypi.python.org/pypi scikit-build

8. When you are confident everything is working correctly, upload the release
    on PyPI:

First, update ``~/.pypirc``:

        [distutils]
        index-servers=
            pypi
            test

        [pypi]
        repository = https://pypi.python.org/pypi
        username = <your production user name goes here>
        password = <your production password goes here>

        [test]
        repository = https://testpypi.python.org/pypi
        username = <your user name goes here>
        password = <your password goes here>

Second, register the project with the PyPI server: ::

        python setup.py register

And upload the release: ::

        twine upload -s dist/scikit*


9.  Add the tagged version to `readthedocs <https://readthedocs.org/projects/scikit-build/>`_.

```

issue_title: The Resource `create` and `load` Aliasing Issue
issue_body:
`Resource` `create` and `load` create 2 aliases for the same object in the callers namespace, _IF_ they're called on the right hand side of an assignment statement.

This leads to confusing errors like this:

https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/issues/407

This "design" issue is being created to provide a single location to discuss the problem and come up with fix.

issue_title: `validate()` doesn't respect the `attribute` parameter
issue_body:
Here's an example:

```
>>> from marshmallow import Schema
>>> from marshmallow import fields
>>> 
>>> class User(Schema):
...     name = fields.String(attribute='first_name', required=True)
...     age = fields.Integer()
... 
>>> u = User()
>>> u.validate({})
{'name': ['Missing data for required field.']}
>>> u.validate({'first_name': 'John'})
{'name': ['Missing data for required field.']}
```

I'm not sure if this is a design decision or a bug, but I would expect `validate()` to respect the `attribute` parameter.

issue_title: Breaking change to load() in marshmallow 3.0.0b7
issue_body:
http://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/upgrading.html#schemas-are-always-strict

"Schema().load and Schema().dump don’t return a (data, errors) duple any more. Only data is returned."

This results in a traceback similar to the following:

```
.../marshmallow/schema.py:542: in validate
    self._do_load(data, many, partial=partial, postprocess=False)
.../marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py:219: in _do_load
    result, errors = super(Schema, self)._do_load(data, many, **kwargs)
E   ValueError: too many values to unpack (expected 2)
```

I did attempt to modify `_do_load()` to only use `errors`, not `result`, but then I got another traceback when it tried to validate a boolean field:

```
.../marshmallow/schema.py:498: in load
    return self._do_load(data, many, partial=partial, postprocess=True)
.../marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py:231: in _do_load
    formatted_messages = self.format_errors(error_messages, many=many)
.../marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py:272: in format_errors
    for message in field_errors
E   TypeError: 'bool' object is not iterable
```

Since I didn't have the TypeError before upgrading marshmallow, there's obviously a little more going on than I know about, having just looked at the code for the first time.

Hi, 
I've recently installed and started playing with Sacred and I'm trying to run an experiment from inside the python shell.
I'm using vscode default shell (a windows shell) and an anaconda environment where I manually installed sacred (pip install sacred) because there is no conda package available yet.

simple.py:

from sacred import Experiment

@ex.config

@ex.named_config

I'm then trying to run it from the python shell with the following commands:

issue_title: Typing (sort-ing) error - bug or expected?
issue_body:
Z3 4.5.1 nightly (1f29cebd4df6) rejects the following program with `unknown constant bar`:

```text
(declare-sort S1)
(declare-sort S2)
(declare-sort S3)

(declare-fun foo (Bool) S1)
(declare-fun foo (Bool) S2)

(declare-fun bar (S2 S2) Bool)
(declare-fun bar (S3 S3) Bool)

(declare-const s2 S2)
(declare-const b Bool)

(assert (bar s2 (foo b))) ;;; ERROR
```

I can see that the overloaded `foo` can give rise to type/sort ambiguities in general, but in this example, I would expect that, in the final `assert`, the type system concludes that `bar: S2 ⨯ S2 → Bool` and `foo: Bool → S2` are to be applied. Is Z3's behaviour expected or is this a bug in the type-checker?

I tried working around the apparent ambiguity using SMT2's `as` terms, but didn't succeed: it seems that only constants can be sort-annotated via `as`. Is there another solution/work-around other than renaming `foo` (e.g. to `fooS1` and `fooS2`)?

issue_title: Add get_filesystem() method
issue_body:
fs provides a great abstraction and useful operations by multiple types of FS objects, a common usage of filesystem is performing many tasks implemented in different functions on one given FS object, like:

```
def high_level_task(directory_path):
    with OSFS(directory_path) as fs:
        low_level_task0(some_args, fs)
        with fs.opendir(sub_dir_path) as sub_fs:
            low_level_task1(some_args, sub_fs)
        low_level_task1(some_args, fs)

def low_level_task0(some_args, fs):
    ... (operations on fs)


def low_level_task1(some_args, fs):
    ... (operations on fs)
```

FS object need to be passed everywhere. But using `with` statement, it should not be a necessary requirement. An example is `variable_scope` in TensorFlow.

Thus if we has an function `get_filesystem()` to get current FS constructed by `with` statement, we can make previous code cleaner like:

```
def high_level_task(directory_path):
    with OSFS(directory_path) as fs:
        low_level_task0(some_args)
        with fs.opendir(sub_dir_path):
            low_level_task1(some_args)
        low_level_task1(some_args)

def low_level_task0(some_args):
    with fs.get_filesystem() as fs:
        ... (operations on fs)

def low_level_task1(some_args):
    with fs.get_filesystem() as fs:
        ... (operations on fs)
```

Further more following functionality may be useful:
 `get_filesystem()` returns default file system when no file system is constructed 
 `get_filesystem(name:str)` returns some other FS object by its name

issue_title: Double URL encoding in issuer_name param to uri_provisioning
issue_body:
#### Version: Latest (v.2.2.5)

The issuer_name parameter is first encoded [here](https://github.com/pyotp/pyotp/blob/master/src/pyotp/utils.py#L64) and the output of that is HTML encoded again [here](https://github.com/pyotp/pyotp/blob/master/src/pyotp/utils.py#L75).

![capture](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9205510/26757534/7207432e-4884-11e7-8746-bb6f06323e68.PNG)

The first filter changes `Hello World` to `Hello%20World` (which is exactly what it should be) but the second time changes the output of the first filter (`Hello%20World`) to `Hello%2520World` (escaping the percent sign before the 20).

When authenticator apps receive this, they interpret the `%25` and render the remainder. Therefore, by using the `uri_provisioning()` method with the `issuer_name` string `"Hello World"`, the apps display `Hello%20World` instead of `Hello World`.

Pull request coming soon.

issue_title: Why should uvcvideo be a module (rather than a built-in)?
issue_body:
My question is not specific to a sensor, but rather the library itself (thus I'm not including the device/model tags at the top of my question).

I noticed that the latest SDK release for NVIDIA Jetson TX1/TX2 boards (which was released only a few days ago) uses kernel v4.4 and the sources already contain the necessary patches for RealSense video formats, etc. 

Right out of the box, I noticed that upon connecting my R200 to my Jetson TX1 board, `dmesg` indicated that the connected camera was correctly recognized: 

> Found UVC 1.10 device Intel RealSense 3D Camera R200

I could also confirm that `uvcvideo` was already available as a builtin. I proceeded with building the latest librealsense source. However, I noticed that upon trying to run any of the samples I would get:

> uvcvideo kernel module is not loaded

I went ahead and built the kernel from the sources, from scratch. Except for forcing `uvcvideo` to be built as a module, I did not touch any of the other settings. Now the camera and the samples work fine without any issues.

Forgive my ignorance (in case this is a stupid question) but is there a reason that `uvcvideo` should be built as a module for librealsense to work?

issue_title: Change Pulp-Smash Code layout to fit Pulp2 and Pulp3 needs.
issue_body:
Code related to Pulp3 will be added to Pulp-Smash soon.

Based on that it does make sense to modify pulp-smash current layout to fit this need. Below, a draft version of future layout .

```
pulp_smash/
├── constants.py
├── pulp2
│   ├── constants.py
│   └── tests
└── pulp3
    ├── constants.py
    └── tests
```

Every project will have its own dir, and it is related files. All the other files there a common for both projects will be in a level above.  For instance, the constant files for Pulp3 will have all related constants of Pulp3. Constants shared by both projects, like pulp-fixtures ones, will be in another constant file a level above.

**This change in layout possibly will impact imports in different parts of the code, and how certain Jenkins Jobs are currently set.**

issue_title: Problem with pmag.dokent() and aniso_magic.py ellipse parameters
issue_body: When running aniso_magic.py with a bootstrap I was getting weird error ellipses that were rotated 90º from the data distribution they were meant to be representing. Datafile: [WT1_anisotropy.txt](https://github.com/PmagPy/PmagPy/files/287506/WT1_anisotropy.txt) Command: `aniso_magic.py -f WT1_anisotropy.txt -par -crd g` Result: ![aniso_test_data](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9516117/15624020/6d7d31c8-244b-11e6-965a-c9dd6f6a6de1.png) ![aniso_test1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9516117/15624022/78e760f6-244b-11e6-8c60-eb134f7a2a9d.png) I eventually worked out that somehow the zeta angle was being given the eta angles D,I parameters and vice-versa, which I traced back to pmag.dokent(): In the first two chunks of highlighted code below there appears to be a mismatch with the third chunk: sigma1at [i][1] in an array is associated with eta and sigma2 at [i][0] with zeta, yet at 3221-3222 the zeta directions are calculated from [i][1] and eta from [i][0]. If you instead have: `3305 sigma1=sigma1+xg[i][0]**2` `3306 sigma2=sigma2+xg[i][1]**2` Then you get the right result. ![aniso_test2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9516117/15624101/2ecc6a46-244d-11e6-9a0e-82267a856753.png) Also: in aniso_magic.py lines 322-325, which print the bootstrap output, the zeta parameters are printed in the eta columns and vice versa.

issue_title: Exposing base member from derived, when base is not wrapped
issue_body:
If I have a base class `B` with a data member `x` (of type `float`, but that is irrelevant) and a derived class `D`. And if I now expose only `D` (because `B` for some other reason should not be exposed) as: `py::class_<D>(m, "D").def(py::init<>()).def_readonly("x", &D::x);` Accessing `x` from Python fails with:

```
E TypeError: (): incompatible function arguments. The following argument types are supported:
E 1. (self: B) -> float
E
E Invoked with: <pybind11_tests.D object at 0x108e2e538>

```

This can be fixed with an explicit cast as in `.def_readonly("x", (float D::*) &D::x);`, but that should not really be required (as pointed out my @jagerman on Gitter: Pybind doesn't know anything about the `D : B` relationship here, which is why it happens. It ought to be possible to make it work properly (and `static_assert` fail when the member isn't a superclass member)).

issue_title: Relationships between models
issue_body:
Right now I'm stuck on the following problem:

We're using the official "memberof" overlay on our OpenLDAP server.

By adding a user to a group the user entry gets a corresponding operational "memberOf" attribute. By design, these "member" and "memberOf" attributes are NOT like some ManyToMany relationship.

If you're going to delete some "memberOf" attribute of a user, the corresponding group will not be updated. This way, you just get some inconsistent relationship, where you can't check if a user is in a group by looking at it's "memberOf" attribute.

Now, to work around this issue it'd be great if there was some way, inside of django-ldapdb, where these cases are being handled.

For me, the final result could look something like this:
```
class LDAPUser(models.Model):
    base_dn = "ou=People,dc=example,dc=org"
    object_classes = ['inetOrgPerson', 'organizationalPerson']

    uid = CharField(db_column='uid', primary_key=True)
    cn = CharField(db_column='cn')
    mail = CharField(db_column='mail')

class LDAPGroup(models.Model):
    base_dn = "ou=Groups,dc=example,dc=org"
    object_classes = ['groupOfNames']

    cn = CharField(db_column='cn', primary_key=True)
    member = ManyToManyField(db_column='member', on_model=LDAPUser)
```
Now, if I wanted to add some LDAPUser to my LDAPGroup, it would have to update the "member" attribute of my group. Seems easy.
But if i wanted to add a new group to my LDAPUser object, it also has to put it's `dn` into the member field of the group.

Maybe there's another way, I don't see yet. Feel free, to enlighten me :)

issue_title: Talisker uses proxy address instead of client address for private endpoint filtering
issue_body:
Clients can access any private endpoint by spoofing the proxy header:

```bash
$ curl https://myservice/_status/metrics -H 'X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1'
# HELP some_metric
# TYPE some_metric counter
some_metric 1.0
```
I'm hitting taliskers's private endpoints behind a reverse proxy (which correctly sets `X-Forwarded-For`). `TALISKER_NETWORKS` is set to the internal network, which includes the proxy itself.

issue_title: Syncing collections with different names
issue_body:
# Problem
Attempting to sync contact collections that have different names

# Description
I am attempting to migrate my contact cards from being stored in iCloud over to my email provider Fastmail. This sounded like a relatively trivial thing to setup but has proven to be much more difficult than i imagined. Going into this my understanding of the problem was that I would simply need to setup a new "remote" source in my config file and create a new sync pair:

```
[pair contacts_list]
a = "address_book_local"
b = "address_book_remote"
collections = ["from a", "from b"]
conflict_resolution = "a wins"

[pair fastmail_contacts_list]
a = "address_book_local"
b = "address_book_remote_fastmail"
collections = ["from a", "from b"]
conflict_resolution = "a wins"

[storage address_book_local]
type = "filesystem"
path = "~/Contacts/"
fileext = ".vcf"

[storage address_book_remote]
type = "carddav"
url = "https://contacts.icloud.com/"
username.fetch = ["command", "..."]
password.fetch = ["command", "..."]

[storage address_book_remote_fastmail]
type = "carddav"
url = "https://carddav.fastmail.com/"
username.fetch = ["command", "..."]
password.fetch = ["command", "..."]
```

My understanding of this is that I am creating a pair to sync from my iCloud account down to a local storage on my filesystem, then use another pair to sync from my filesystem up to my Fastmail account. This *seems* to work, however when I login to the Fastmail web-portal I don't see any of my contacts. My suspicion is that something is being synced but I won't see that because Fastmail has a default collection called `Default` whereas the default collection on iCloud is called `card`. I am trying to figure out if there is any way I can get these two collections to mesh with each other (even just once) so I can do the migration then remove data from my iCloud account. I have a strong desire to get this to work, because my alternative is to use the importer that Fastmail has which only allows me to import one `.vcf` file at a time.

# Info

* vdirsyncer: `version 0.15.0`
* OS: `OS X 10.12.5 (16F73)`
* Python: `v3.6.1`

issue_title: Certbot crashes if EFF e-mail submission endpoint is unavailable
issue_body:
Certbot will crash if the user's e-mail information can't be submitted to the EFF endpoint:

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/issue-when-trying-to-create-certificate/59307/1

https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/eff.py#L74

It crashes with a somewhat relevant error if the endpoint is unavailable, but a confusing error if the endpoint returns something other than JSON. It should probably not crash at all in either case. If the endpoint specifically says that the user's e-mail address is malformed, that's probably worth stopping to report to the user, but if the endpoint simply doesn't reply usefully, it's probably preferable to continue since I wouldn't think we really want to make users wait in this situation.

(Adding the "bug" tag at least for the case where the endpoint returns a non-JSON reply because the error shown to the user in this case is unhelpful.)

issue_title: Docker, change from entrypoint to cmd
issue_body:
Currently the Docker image of certbot (certbot/certbot) is using `ENTRYPOINT [ "certbot" ]`.

The advantage is that you can then run a certbot command like this:

`docker run -it --rm --name certbot certbot/certbot certonly --args`

However, it also means that you cannot run a shell inside the container like this:

`docker run -it --rm --name certbot certbot/certbot sh`

Which makes using certbot very difficult as you have to invoke the docker container again and again, especially when having to update DNS entries for every try. It is also difficult to get the certificates out from the container, as the only way is to mount volumes to the host. 1) This is very flaky on Windows. 2) There is also some redirection in the Dockerfile (`VOLUME /etc/letsencrypt /var/lib/letsencrypt`) which means that the output from the container logs are wrong.

So, please consider something like this:

```
- ENTRYPOINT [ "certbot" ]
+ CMD [ "/bin/sh", "-c" ]
```

That way you can run it non-interactively (like now):

`docker run -it --rm --name certbot certbot/certbot "certbot certonly --args"` (Even works on Windows!).

And "interactively" via a shell:

`docker run -it --rm --name certbot certbot/certbot sh`

Not sure about the `VOLUME` statement. I think the paths could be cleaned up to avoid confusion.

issue_title: #Feature: Grouping Metrics.
issue_labels: [{'id': 545176616, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/deep-compute/basescript/labels/Effort:%20High', 'name': 'Effort: High', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 545176654, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/deep-compute/basescript/labels/Impact:%20High', 'name': 'Impact: High', 'color': '1d76db', 'default': False}, {'id': 545181822, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/deep-compute/basescript/labels/Status:%20In%20progress', 'name': 'Status: In progress', 'color': 'ededed', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
#### Read-Me:
* After implementing the `metric` structure(https://github.com/deep-compute/basescript/issues/49) in the `Basescript` we lost a feature called `Grouping-Metrics` so we implemented that feature for the `metrics` in this release.
* We modified the `Basescript` code to support `Django` logger as well.
* The default grouping `time_interval=1sec` we can modify as per our requirement in your code.
* The grouping metric contains new keys called `num` it tells about the total number of records grouped in  1 sec. and the key user had specified the value should be the `average_value`.
#### Sample-code:
```python
from basescript import BaseScript
import time
import random

class Stats(BaseScript):
    def __init__(self):
        super(Stats, self).__init__()

    def run(self):
        ts = time.time()
        while True:
            # New Metric Format.
            self.log.info("server_stats", company="deepcompute", user="rajendra",
                time_duration=(time.time()-ts), type="metric", random_number=random.randint(1, 50))

if __name__ == '__main__':
    Stats().start()
```
#### Sample Grouping-Metric:
![screen shot 2018-03-15 at 6 22 50 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16955073/37464291-e4526894-287d-11e8-8220-832a28d26078.png)

issue_title: Need ABS around FARM_FINGERPRINT
issue_body:
FARM_FINGERPRINT returns both +ve and -ve numbers, so usage such as:

WHERE MOD(FARM_FINGERPRINT(CAST(hashmonth AS STRING)), 100) < 1

(from %bq sample) is wrong.  It will return 51% of the data, not just 1%.

(I submitted a PR for this: maybe it's just not made it into release)

issue_title: Raiden Client not compatible with Infura
issue_body:
## Problem Definition
I am considering the possiblity of using a 3rd-party hosted Ethereum Full Node and decided to go with [Infura](https://infura.io/). This allows a small IoT Device to run the Raiden Client and interact with 3rd-party node. 

Reason is: not everybody has infrastructure to host an own full node, ensure it's 24/7 uptime, maintaining security updates and so on. 

The infura documentation regarding the available API Endpoints is shown [here](https://infura.io/docs/api/get/symbolFull). 

It covers a variety of methods of rpcapis: `eth_*, net_*, web3_*`

The problem is: when calling raiden with `--eth-rpc-endpoint https://ropsten.infura.io/<myToken>:<myPort>` it produces the error message:

```
Could not contact the ethereum node through JSON-RPC.
Please make sure that JSON-RPC is enabled for these interfaces:

    eth_*, net_*, web3_*

geth: https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/wiki/Management-APIs
```

It would now be interesting to identify if there are actually necessary methods which are not provided by Infura's API endpoints.

### System Description

Ubuntu 16.04 LTS
Raiden-0.4.1-linux
Geth on Infura: Geth/v1.8.3-stable/linux-amd64/go1.10

issue_title: Eclipse errors and warnings when importing DICOM set, generated from TRiP files format 1.4
issue_body:
![error_jet03](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/22157203/23257784/9e3d95aa-f9c5-11e6-8844-6f4458f16b1f.png)
I was trying to test different formats of TRiP files (.vdx,.hed,.ctx) being converted into DICOM using pytrip 1.3.1 in relation to #245. 
I had some files in format 1.4 originally converted from DICOM into TRiP format around two years ago using the then-version of pytrip. When I converted these back into DICOM and imported them in Eclipse, I got the above shown errors and warnings and the import failed (there are more warnings not seen in the screen window but they are identical to the last couple of warnings). It is the first time I see these errors. Normally we just get some warnings and the import works.

To recap, the difference between the files tested until now and the warnings/errors they give: 
1) Niels' are generated from TRiP format 2.0. Where they originally DICOM? No warnings, no errors, import works.
2) Alinas files are generated from TRiP format 1.2 from DICOM originally converted into TRiP format using dcm2trip. Some warnings #255, no errors, import works.    
3) In this case files are generated from TRiP format 1.4 from DICOM originally converted into TRiP format using an older 2015 version of pytripgui. We get warnings, errors and the import fails. 
An important note is that the original DICOM Alina uses and the ones I use are from the same place. Only the TRiP format (and the way they were made into this format) are different.

Now it remains to be figured out if this issue comes from the difference in format or if the older version of pytrip had bugs when generating TRiP files?

    pass

class Soda(Supply):
    pass

class Consumption():

I can create Consumptions with Water, Beer or Soda, but how  can I filter it?
Something like this:

issue_title: Tune trial-resources is named trial_resources in code
issue_body:
<!--
General questions should be asked on the mailing list ray-dev@googlegroups.com.

Before submitting an issue, please fill out the following form.

### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution**:
Linux Ubuntu 16.04
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**:
binary
- **Ray version**:
0.5.0
- **Python version**:
2.7.14
- **Exact command to reproduce**:
Add the following to tune/examples/pbt_example.py:
```
    run_experiments(
        {
            "pbt_test": {
                "run": "my_class",
                "trial-resources": {"cpu": 4, "gpu": 1},
                "stop": {
                    "training_iteration": 2 if args.smoke_test else 99999
                },
                "repeat": 1,
                "config": {
                    "factor_1": 4.0,
                    "factor_2": 1.0,
                },
            }
        },
        scheduler=pbt,
        verbose=False)
```
<!--
You can obtain the Ray version with

python -c "import ray; print(ray.__version__)"
-->

### Describe the problem

tune argument parser expects "trial-resources", but the in variant_generator "trial_resources" is expected.

issue_title: Possible collision of different functions
issue_body:
This code shows that different functions can collide in ray:

```python
class A(object):
  def func(self):
    return inspect.getframeinfo(inspect.currentframe()).lineno

class B(object):
  def func(self):
    return inspect.getframeinfo(inspect.currentframe()).lineno

t1 = ray.remote(A.func)
t2 = ray.remote(B.func)

print(ray.get(t1.remote(None)))
print(ray.get(t2.remote(None)))
```

This happens because in `worker.py`, the signature of function is calculated like this:

```python
 def remote_function_decorator(func, function_properties):
   func_name = "{}.{}".format(func.__module__, func.__name__)
   if func_id is None:
     function_id = compute_function_id(func_name, func)
```

So there's nothing about which class the function stays in.

issue_title: Using `pk` to set the value of a primary key using `on_conflict` does not work
issue_body:
```
class MyModel(PostgresModel):
    myfield = models.CharField(max_length=255)

obj1 = (
    MyModel.objects
    .on_conflict(['pk'], ConflictAction.UPDATE)
    .insert_and_get(pk=0, myfield='beer')
)

obj2 = (
    MyModel.objects
    .on_conflict(['pk'], ConflictAction.UPDATE)
    .insert_and_get(pk=0, myfield='beer')
)

assert obj1.id == obj2.id
```

The following example does not work. This is because `pk` refers to the a `models.AutoField`, which django-postgres-extra does special logic for. However, it should be possible to override the default value. In the example above, you should end up with 1 row with `id=0`. However, instead, you end up with to rows with `id=1` and `id=2`.

This is closely related to #23.

issue_title: test_SocketType_resolve fails
issue_body:
Apparently on Mac, on *some* setups, `getaddrinfo()` will always return IPv4 mapped addresses regardless of whether the AI_V4MAPPED is actually set. This causes failures in this assertion https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/fd0d8770a120a09a506ccd8a3f9969b26a22f38e/trio/tests/test_socket.py#L420-L422

This is probably a system configuration issue and maybe there is not much we can do about it. But it's causing our tests to fail on some machines. I've seen this happen regularly on my work laptop for some time (macOS 10.13.5, Python 3.6 and 3.7) and a couple other sprinters at EuroPython had the same issue. Good news is that it doesn't affect our Mac CI.

One potential (maybe not ideal) solution is to verify whether the system has this quirk and in that case skip the test.

### Details

Calling getaddrinfo() on an IPv4 address, with AF_INET6 and the AI_V4MAPPED flag returns an IPv4-mapped IPv6 address. This is documented and works in all platforms.

```python
>>> getaddrinfo('1.2.3.4', 0, AF_INET6, SOCK_STREAM, 0, AI_V4MAPPED)
[(<AddressFamily.AF_INET6: 30>, <SocketKind.SOCK_STREAM: 1>, 6, '', ('::ffff:1.2.3.4', 0, 0, 0))]
```

If I only want IPv6 addresses I won't set the AI_V4MAPPED and I won't get any result back. For example, this is what happens on Linux:

```
>>> s.getaddrinfo('1.2.3.4', 0, s.AF_INET6, s.SOCK_STREAM, 0, 0)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.7/socket.py", line 748, in getaddrinfo
    for res in _socket.getaddrinfo(host, port, family, type, proto, flags):
socket.gaierror: [Errno -9] Address family for hostname not supported
```

But if I try the same on my Mac I still get the IPv4-mapped results, even though I shouldn't!

```python
>>> getaddrinfo('1.2.3.4', 0, AF_INET6, SOCK_STREAM, 0, 0)
[(<AddressFamily.AF_INET6: 30>, <SocketKind.SOCK_STREAM: 1>, 6, '', ('::ffff:1.2.3.4', 0, 0, 0))]
```

issue_title: Inconsistent licence files
issue_body:
Following https://github.com/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/268#issuecomment-318916583

In 
https://github.com/python-windrose/windrose/blob/master/setup.py
it's `License :: OSI Approved :: CEA CNRS Inria Logiciel Libre License, version 2.1 (CeCILL-2.1)'`

but in https://github.com/python-windrose/windrose/blob/master/LICENCE
and in https://github.com/python-windrose/windrose/blob/master/windrose/version.py#L5
it's CeCILL-B

In https://pypi.python.org/pypi?%3Aaction=list_classifiers
CeCILL-2.1 is the only CeCILL license (moreover it's OSI approved)
so if we change to use CeCILL-B everywhere in this project we need to know what should be written in `setup.py` (maybe only its [SPDX](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SPDX): `CeCILL-B`)

@LionelR Should we update update `setup.py` or `LICENSE` (and `version.py`)?

I noticed that some other projects have same "problem".

https://github.com/d9pouces/django-floor
https://github.com/akaszynski/PyACVD
...

CeCILL-2.1 is a GPL compatible french license
CeCILL-B License is a BSD compatible french license (GPL incompatible)

https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Licence_CeCILL

CeCILL-B imposes author(s) citation not CeCILL-2.1

issue_title: S3 Not responding to OPTIONS request when using presigned URL from the browser
issue_body:
I'm attempting to use a pre-signed URL from within the browser to upload to my localstack S3 service. Generating the URL itself is trivial, however when attempting to POST to the resulting url (e.g `http://localstack:4572/ps.local/test?X-Amz-Content-Sha256=UNSIGNED-PAYLOAD...`), the browser makes an OPTIONS preflight request, to which localstack does not respond.

It appears as if there is no way around this even with flexible CORS rules (see below). It's not therefore possible to directly upload files from the browser to localstack S3 using presigned URL's.
```
            $s3->putBucketCors([
                'Bucket' => 'myBucket',
                'CORSConfiguration' => [
                    'CORSRules' => [
                        [
                            'AllowedOrigins' => ['*'],
                            'AllowedMethods' => ['POST', 'GET', 'PUT'],
                            'MaxAgeSeconds' => 3000,
                            'AllowedHeaders' => ['*']

issue_title: Client id always included in body of token request
issue_body:
I use the WebApplication client and in [`prepare_request_body`](https://github.com/idan/oauthlib/blob/master/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/clients/web_application.py#L88-L89) I noticed the `client_id` parameter is never used. However [`self.client_id`](https://github.com/idan/oauthlib/blob/master/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/clients/web_application.py#L127-L128) is always passed down to `prepare_token_request` and thus always included in the body.

An unused parameter doesn't look good but maybe this is intended, I've only quickly read the RFC 6749. If this is the case it should at least be stated in the docstring.

The server here (I guess OpenAM) rejects queries when they contain the client_id in the body.

issue_title: Call for votes: Method signatures
issue_body:
We want the API to stay consistent for a long time. But we know that the method signatures (i.e. the amount, names, types or if they're optional) of parameters will change.

One example is the change from user_id to a possible user_email from plone3 to plone4 which affects most user method signatures. They all have to add user_email as a new (optional) parameter. More of these changes will come.

With that in mind, we have two choices:

1) Only Keyword Arguments with manual tests (see below *t1*)
2) Only Positional Arguments (see below *t2*)

```python
def t1(**kw):
    """ Does something with an obj.

    :param obj: [required] The object
    :type obj: object
    """
    if not kw.get('obj'):
        raise ValueError("obj is required")


def t2(obj):
    """ Does something with an obj.

    :param obj: [required] The object
    :type obj: object
    """
    pass
```

The advantage with *t1* would be that we can add keyword arguments in future versions for BBB easily.
The advantage with *t2* is that code inspection can find the method signature (e.g. ipython) and displays the arguments nicely. Also it feels more pythonic.

The disadvantage with *t1* is that you have to read the docstring in order to know about the parameters. Code inspection will not help.
The disadvantage with *t2* is that people might start to use the method with positional arguments instead of keyword arguments which will make BBB more complicated. We'd have to make clear that people can expect BBB only if they use keyword arguments.

issue_title: Nest expectations on return values
issue_body:
In python-mock library, return_value can be used to chain expectations (as it is also a mock), example:

    def test_mongo_db_api_connects_to_mongo(self):
        with mock.patch('pymongo.Connection') as mock_conn:
            stub_cursor = "whatever"
            mock_conn.return_value.__getitem__ = mock.MagicMock()
            mock_conn.return_value.__getitem__.return_value.__getitem__.return_value = stub_cursor

            api = MongoDbApi(database_name='dbname', collection_name='collection')
            self.assertEquals(api.cursor, stub_cursor)

            mock_conn.assert_called_with({'host':'localhost'})
            mock_conn.return_value.__getitem__.assert_called_with('dbname')
            mock_conn.return_value.__getitem__.return_value.__getitem__.assert_called_with('collection')

would be nice if something similar was available in chai, maybe like this:

    def test_mongo_db_api_connects_to_mongo(self):
        stub_cursor = stub("whatever")
        expect(pymongo.Connection).args({'host':'localhost'}).\
                return_value.expect('__getitem__').args('dbname').\
                return_value.expect('__getitem__').args('collection').\
                returns( stub_cursor )
        api = MongoDbApi(database_name='dbname', collection_name='collection')
        assert_equals(api.cursor, stub_cursor)

It does behave a bit different though, since the object that receives the method now has an .expect() method which is different from how Chai usually sets expectations on objects, but on other hand it looks so cool! :)
